note: ResearchBench (arXiv 2503.21248v3). Blind digest + Layer2/3 adjudication done against full text. Manuscript makes 1 claim (C109). Both sub-claims verified true against the paper: (1) it restricts to papers published from 2024 onward to control contamination; (2) it evaluates an inspiration-based 3-sub-task decomposition (inspiration retrieval / hypothesis composition / hypothesis ranking) and establishes NO expert/human performance baseline. The paper's experts (5 PhD students, 62 papers) validate only the extraction/decomposition ACCURACY (91.9% major / 82.3% all), never task performance. The word 'baseline' appears once in the paper (App. A.1 l.1908) and refers to 'baseline model cutoff dates', not an expert baseline — confirming no expert-baseline exists to close. The 3-sub-task 'sufficient set' is imported from MOOSE-Chem (Yang et al. 2025b), not original to ResearchBench, but C109 does not claim otherwise. Minor lexical nuance: paper says '2024 onward'/'2024 or later' (inclusive) while manuscript writes 'post-2024 findings'; non-material since the contamination-strict subset is explicitly 'after July 2024' and the substantive point is faithful.

C109.manuscript_text: Constructing the contamination-controlled benchmark and the expert baseline is itself an open task; a recent benchmark takes a step by restricting to post-2024 findings to control contamination~\citep{liu2025researchbench}, though it evaluates inspiration-based decomposition rather than closing the expert-baseline gap.
evidence: (a) post-2024 contamination control — Abstract l.29-31: 'To prevent data contamination, we focus exclusively on publications from 2024 onward, ensuring minimal overlap with LLM pretraining data'; Body l.159: 'To prevent data contamination, we select only papers published from 2024 onward'; l.371-374: 'we apply the agentic framework only to papers published in 2024 or later, thereby minimizing overlap with the pretraining data of LLMs'; strict subset App. A.1 l.1906-1908: 'includes only papers published after July 2024 ... farther away from the baseline model cutoff dates'.

(b) inspiration-based decomposition — Title l.2: 'via Inspiration-Based Task Decomposition'; Abstract l.24-25: 'a sufficient set of scientific discovery sub-tasks—inspiration retrieval, hypothesis composition, and hypothesis ranking'; Sec 3.1 l.277-284 formalizes the decomposition (Eqs 1-4).

(c) does NOT close an expert-baseline gap — experts appear ONLY to validate extraction accuracy: l.150-156 '...check whether the decomposition is accurate ... decomposition accuracy was 91.9% ... 82.3%'; App. A.2 l.2057 'They check the accuracy of the extracted research questions, inspirations, and hypotheses'. No human-vs-LLM performance comparison anywhere; the lone occurrence of 'baseline' (l.1908) denotes model cutoff dates, not an expert baseline. Novelty claim is inferred from OOD inspiration-retrieval Hit Ratio (GPT-4o 45.65% at top-4%, Table 2b l.833-834), not benchmarked against experts.
C109.nuance: Mild imprecision: paper's window is '2024 onward' (inclusive) whereas manuscript writes 'post-2024'; and 'findings' stands in for 'papers/publications'. Neither changes meaning — the contamination-strict subset is literally post-July-2024, and the characterization of what the benchmark does vs. does not do is faithful. Borderline IMPRECISE-OK but the substantive claim is accurate.
C109.fix: None required. Optional (LOW): change 'post-2024 findings' to 'papers published from 2024 onward' to mirror the paper's exact window; purely cosmetic.

reverse_findings.fact: The 3-sub-task 'sufficient set' decomposition (inspiration retrieval / hypothesis composition / hypothesis ranking) is imported from MOOSE-Chem (Yang et al. 2025b), not originated by ResearchBench.
reverse_findings.mismatch: None — C109 says the benchmark 'evaluates inspiration-based decomposition', which is exactly right and does not attribute invention to ResearchBench.

reverse_findings.fact: Expert evaluation (5 PhD students; 62 sampled papers) is used solely to validate extraction/decomposition ACCURACY (91.9% major-only / 82.3% all issues), not to establish a human performance baseline on any of the three tasks.
reverse_findings.mismatch: None — this directly SUPPORTS the manuscript's assertion that the paper does not close the expert-baseline gap.

reverse_findings.fact: Paper's contamination window is '2024 onward' / '2024 or later' (2024 inclusive); a stricter re-run uses papers 'after July 2024'. Strict vs Original results are 'very close ... consistent with normal sample variance'.
reverse_findings.mismatch: Minor lexical — manuscript's 'post-2024' reads as after-2024 while the main benchmark is inclusive of 2024. Non-material; the strict subset is literally post-July-2024.

reverse_findings.fact: Headline empirical result: LLMs excel at OOD inspiration retrieval (most models ~80% at 20% retained; GPT-4o 45.65% at 4% retained); composition and ranking are only moderate ('none of the models achieve consistently high performance').
reverse_findings.mismatch: None — C109 does not cite these numbers; no conflict.

summary.evidence: post-2024 (l.29-31/159/373), inspiration-based 3-sub-task decomposition (title/l.24-25/277-284), no expert performance baseline (experts validate extraction accuracy only, l.150-156/2057; 'baseline' at l.1908 = model cutoff dates).
summary.fix: None required; optional cosmetic rewording of 'post-2024 findings' -> 'papers published from 2024 onward'.
